title: PLE English 2024
subject: English
description: Uganda National Examinations Board Primary Leaving Examination 2024 English paper.

questions.questionText: Rose crossed the road as __________ as it was clear.
questions.instruction: In each of the questions 1 to 5, fill in the blank space with a suitable word.

parts.answer: soon
acceptable_answers: immediately (less precise)
quickly (not best fit)
parts.explanation: Tutor: Let’s read this carefully. “Rose crossed the road as ___ as it was clear.” What structure do you see? Student: “as … as” Tutor: Good. Now this structure is often used with time expressions. What is the full idea here? Student: She crossed when it became clear. Tutor: Exactly. So we need a word that means “immediately after.” What fits? Student: soon. Tutor: Yes. “as soon as it was clear” means she did not delay at all. Tutor: Why not “quickly”? Student: Because it doesn’t fit the structure. Tutor: Correct. The correct expression is as soon as.

questions.questionText: Adam is two years __________ than his little sister.
questions.instruction: In each of the questions 1 to 5, fill in the blank space with a suitable word.

parts.answer: older
acceptable_answers: elder (incorrect in this structure)
parts.explanation: Tutor: This sentence is comparing Adam and his sister. What word tells you that? Student: “than” Tutor: Exactly. “Than” signals comparison. So what form do we need? Student: A comparative form. Tutor: Correct. Now we have two choices many students confuse: “older” and “elder.” Which one works with “than”? Student: older. Tutor: Right. “Older than” is correct. “Elder” is usually used without “than,” for example: “my elder brother.” Tutor: So the correct answer is older.

questions.questionText: The last bus leaves __________ Kampala at 12 noon.
questions.instruction: In each of the questions 1 to 5, fill in the blank space with a suitable word.

parts.answer: for
acceptable_answers: to (not best fit in this structure)
parts.explanation: Tutor: Let’s think about direction. Is the bus going to Kampala or leaving Kampala? Student: It is going to Kampala. Tutor: Good. When something is going towards a place, which preposition do we use with “leave”? Student: for? Tutor: Exactly. We say “leaves for Kampala.” Tutor: What would “leaves Kampala” mean? Student: That it is leaving from Kampala. Tutor: Correct. So to show destination, we use for.

questions.questionText: Kibisi looked smart after combing his __________.
questions.instruction: In each of the questions 1 to 5, fill in the blank space with a suitable word.

parts.answer: hair
parts.explanation: Tutor: Let’s picture the situation. What makes someone look neat or smart? Student: Being well dressed? Tutor: Yes, and also grooming. What do we comb? Student: Hair. Tutor: Correct. Now think—can we say “combing his hairs”? Student: No. Tutor: Right. “Hair” is uncountable in this context. So the correct answer is hair.

questions.questionText: I __________ my prayers whenever I wake up in the morning.
questions.instruction: In each of the questions 1 to 5, fill in the blank space with a suitable word.

parts.answer: say
acceptable_answers: recite
parts.explanation: Tutor: What is the natural expression we use with prayers? Student: We pray? Tutor: Yes, but look at the sentence: “I ___ my prayers.” What verb fits that structure? Student: say? Tutor: Correct. We say “say my prayers.” Tutor: Could “recite” also work? Student: Yes. Tutor: Good. But the most common and expected answer is say.

questions.questionText: Anne is the __________ of the two girls. (fast)
questions.instruction: In each of the questions 6 to 15, use the correct form of the word given in brackets.

parts.answer: faster
parts.explanation: Tutor: We are comparing two girls. When we compare two things, what form do we use? Student: Comparative. Tutor: Exactly. Now the word given is “fast.” What is the comparative form? Student: faster. Tutor: Correct. Now here is the tricky part—some students may think “fastest.” Why is that wrong? Student: Because that is for more than two. Tutor: Well done. Since there are only two girls, the correct answer is faster.

questions.questionText: The big mangoes Aisha bought were sweet and __________. (juice)
questions.instruction: In each of the questions 6 to 15, use the correct form of the word given in brackets.

parts.answer: juicy
parts.explanation: Tutor: The sentence describes mangoes. What type of word do we need after “sweet and”? Student: An adjective. Tutor: Correct. Now “juice” is a noun. What is its adjective form? Student: juicy. Tutor: Exactly. “Sweet and juicy” describes the mangoes. Tutor: Always check the form of the word needed in the sentence.

questions.questionText: I was glad to hear that the visitors arrived home __________. (safe)
questions.instruction: In each of the questions 6 to 15, use the correct form of the word given in brackets.

parts.answer: safely
parts.explanation: Tutor: This sentence tells us how the visitors arrived. What type of word describes “how” something happens? Student: An adverb. Tutor: Correct. Now “safe” is an adjective. What is the adverb form? Student: safely. Tutor: Exactly. So we say “arrived home safely.” Tutor: A common mistake is writing “safe,” but that is not correct here.

questions.questionText: Obe could not hide his __________ when he was elected head boy. (happy)
questions.instruction: In each of the questions 6 to 15, use the correct form of the word given in brackets.

parts.answer: happiness
acceptable_answers: joy (contextual)
excitement
parts.explanation: Tutor: Look at the structure: “could not hide his ___.” What type of word is needed? Student: A noun. Tutor: Correct. Now the given word is “happy,” which is an adjective. What is the noun form? Student: happiness. Tutor: Exactly. That fits perfectly in the sentence. Tutor: Other words like “joy” could make sense, but the question wants a form of “happy.”

questions.questionText: Does Jojo enjoy going out on __________ days? (rain)
questions.instruction: In each of the questions 6 to 15, use the correct form of the word given in brackets.

parts.answer: rainy
parts.explanation: Tutor: We are describing “days.” What type of word do we need? Student: An adjective. Tutor: Correct. Now the word given is “rain.” What is the adjective form? Student: rainy. Tutor: Exactly. “Rainy days.” Tutor: Be careful—not “raining days.” That is not correct here.

questions.questionText: We have been __________ English since Primary One. (learn)
questions.instruction: In each of the questions 6 to 15, use the correct form of the word given in brackets.

parts.answer: learning
parts.explanation: Tutor: Let’s read the sentence carefully: “We have been ___ English since Primary One.” Which part tells you the action started in the past and is still continuing? Student: “have been” Tutor: Good. And the word “since” also helps us. What tense usually goes with “have been” plus a continuing action? Student: The continuous form? Tutor: Exactly. We need the present perfect continuous form. Tutor: So from the word “learn,” what do we write? Student: learning. Tutor: Correct. The full sentence becomes: “We have been learning English since Primary One.” Tutor: A common mistake is to write “learnt” or “learned,” but that would not fit after “have been” in this sentence.

questions.questionText: A friend of __________ won a prize during the spelling bee. (my)
questions.instruction: In each of the questions 6 to 15, use the correct form of the word given in brackets.

parts.answer: mine
parts.explanation: Tutor: This one tests pronouns. Look at the sentence: “A friend of ___ won a prize.” Can we say “a friend of my”? Student: No. Tutor: Correct. Why not? Student: Because “my” must come before a noun. Tutor: Exactly. “My” is a possessive adjective, like in “my book.” But here, the noun “friend” is already there, so we need the possessive pronoun. Tutor: What is the possessive pronoun form of “my”? Student: mine. Tutor: Correct. So the answer is mine.

questions.questionText: Dr. Aleper encouraged us to start our __________ early enough so as to perform well. (revise)
questions.instruction: In each of the questions 6 to 15, use the correct form of the word given in brackets.

parts.answer: revision
acceptable_answers: revising (less suitable)
studies (not from given word)
parts.explanation: Tutor: Let’s look at what the sentence needs. “Start our ___ early enough.” What type of word fits after “our” here? Student: A noun? Tutor: Exactly. The word given is “revise,” which is a verb. We need its noun form. Student: revision. Tutor: Correct. “Start our revision early enough” is the correct expression. Tutor: Why not “revise”? Student: Because that is a verb. Tutor: Right. We need a thing or activity name, not an action word.

questions.questionText: Gimbo was __________ by mosquitoes because she did not sleep under a treated mosquito net. (bite)
questions.instruction: In each of the questions 6 to 15, use the correct form of the word given in brackets.

parts.answer: bitten
parts.explanation: Tutor: Let’s examine the sentence carefully. “Gimbo was ___ by mosquitoes.” Which part tells you this is in the passive voice? Student: “was” Tutor: Correct. In passive voice, after “was,” we usually use which form of the verb? Student: Past participle. Tutor: Exactly. Now the verb given is “bite.” What is the past participle? Student: bitten. Tutor: Correct. So the sentence becomes: “Gimbo was bitten by mosquitoes.” Tutor: A common mistake is writing “bited,” but that is wrong because “bite” is an irregular verb.

questions.questionText: One of the __________ has been sold to the farmer. (puppy)
questions.instruction: In each of the questions 6 to 15, use the correct form of the word given in brackets.

parts.answer: puppies
parts.explanation: Tutor: This one tests plural forms. Let’s look at the expression “one of the ___.” Does that take a singular or plural noun? Student: Plural. Tutor: Correct. Even though we say “one,” the noun after “of the” is plural. Tutor: Now what is the plural of “puppy”? Student: puppies. Tutor: Exactly. Why do we change “y” to “ies”? Student: Because there is a consonant before the y. Tutor: Very good. So the correct answer is puppies.

questions.questionText: Write the short form kg in full.
questions.instruction: In each of the questions 16 to 17, write the given short forms in full.

parts.answer: kilogram
acceptable_answers: kilogramme (older spelling)
parts.explanation: Tutor: What does “kg” stand for in measurement? Student: Kilogram. Tutor: Correct. It is used for measuring mass or weight. Tutor: Sometimes you may see the older spelling “kilogramme,” but today kilogram is the more common form.

questions.questionText: Write the short form they’ve in full.
questions.instruction: In each of the questions 16 to 17, write the given short forms in full.

parts.answer: they have
parts.explanation: Tutor: This is a contraction. What does the apostrophe show? Student: That some letters are missing. Tutor: Correct. In “they’ve,” what has been shortened? Student: have. Tutor: Exactly. So “they’ve” becomes they have. Tutor: Be careful not to write “they are.” That would be the short form “they’re,” not “they’ve.”

questions.questionText: Arrange the following words in alphabetical order: plank, nail, saw, hammer.
questions.instruction: In each of the questions 16 to 17, write the given short forms in full.

parts.answer: hammer, nail, plank, saw
parts.explanation: Tutor: To arrange words alphabetically, what do we compare first? Student: The first letters. Tutor: Good. Let’s list them: - plank → p - nail → n - saw → s - hammer → h Tutor: Which comes first in the alphabet? Student: h Tutor: Good. Then? Student: n, then p, then s. Tutor: Exactly. So the correct order is hammer, nail, plank, saw. Tutor: Always move step by step. Don’t rush.

questions.questionText: Arrange the following words in alphabetical order: eye, nose, ear, neck.
questions.instruction: In each of the questions 18 to 19, arrange the following words in alphabetical order.

parts.answer: ear, eye, neck, nose
parts.explanation: Tutor: Again, let’s compare the first letters. - eye → e - nose → n - ear → e - neck → n Tutor: Which letter comes first? Student: e. Tutor: Good. Now between “ear” and “eye,” both begin with e. So what do we compare next? Student: The next letters. Tutor: Correct. “ear” has a, while “eye” has y. Since a comes before y, which comes first? Student: ear. Tutor: Exactly. Now for “neck” and “nose,” both begin with n. Compare the second letters: e and o. Which comes first? Student: e. Tutor: So the final order is ear, eye, neck, nose.

questions.questionText: Rewrite the sentence giving the opposite of the underlined word: Mary forgot to turn off the TV last night.
questions.instruction: In each of the questions 18 to 19, arrange the following words in alphabetical order.

parts.answer: Mary remembered to turn off the TV last night.
parts.explanation: Tutor: We are told to give the opposite of the underlined word. Which word is underlined? Student: forgot. Tutor: Good. What is the opposite of “forgot”? Student: remembered. Tutor: Correct. So we replace only that word and keep the rest of the sentence the same. Tutor: What is the new sentence? Student: Mary remembered to turn off the TV last night. Tutor: Excellent. Always change only the word asked, unless the whole sentence structure must change.

questions.questionText: Rewrite the sentence giving the opposite of the underlined word: Some foreign tourists like carrying light bags.
questions.instruction: In each of the questions 20 to 21, rewrite the sentence giving the opposite of the underlined word.

parts.answer: Some foreign tourists like carrying heavy bags.
acceptable_answers: big bags (not best)
weighty bags (less natural)
parts.explanation: Tutor: Which word is underlined here? Student: light. Tutor: Good. Now, in this sentence, does “light” mean brightness or not heavy? Student: Not heavy. Tutor: Exactly. So what is the opposite of “light” in that meaning? Student: heavy. Tutor: Correct. So the new sentence becomes: “Some foreign tourists like carrying heavy bags.” Tutor: This is very important. In English, one word can have different meanings, so you must first understand the meaning used in the sentence.

questions.questionText: Rearrange the words to form a correct sentence: strangers gifts never from accept.
questions.instruction: In each of the questions 20 to 21, rewrite the sentence giving the opposite of the underlined word.

parts.answer: Never accept gifts from strangers.
acceptable_answers: Accept gifts from strangers never. (wrong structure)
parts.explanation: Tutor: Let’s arrange the words into a proper sentence. Which word would sound right at the beginning as a warning? Student: Never. Tutor: Good. Warnings often begin with “Never.” What should come next? Student: accept. Tutor: Correct. Then what is being accepted? Student: gifts. Tutor: Yes. And from whom? Student: from strangers. Tutor: Excellent. So the full sentence is Never accept gifts from strangers. Tutor: This also makes good sense as advice.

questions.questionText: Rearrange the words to form a correct sentence: which is class in cousin your?
questions.instruction: In each of the questions 22 to 23, rearrange the words to form a correct sentence.

parts.answer: Which class is your cousin in?
acceptable_answers: Your cousin is in which class? (less natural in school grammar context)
parts.explanation: Tutor: Is this a statement or a question? Student: A question. Tutor: Correct. So the sentence should end with a question mark. Now, which word usually starts this kind of question? Student: Which. Tutor: Good. Then what comes after it? Student: class. Tutor: Yes. Then we need the verb “is.” After that? Student: your cousin. Tutor: Exactly. And since we are asking about the class he or she is in, we end with “in.” Tutor: So the correct sentence is Which class is your cousin in? Tutor: Always check that the order sounds natural in spoken English.

questions.questionText: Give the plural of the given word: ox
questions.instruction: In each of the questions 22 to 23, rearrange the words to form a correct sentence.

parts.answer: oxen
parts.explanation: Tutor: Most plurals in English take “s” or “es,” but some are irregular. Is “ox” a regular plural? Student: No. Tutor: Correct. Its plural is oxen. Tutor: This is one of those special words you must remember, just like “child” becomes “children.”

questions.questionText: Give the plural of the given word: secretary
questions.instruction: In each of the questions 24 to 25, give the plural of the given word.

parts.answer: secretaries
parts.explanation: Tutor: To make “secretary” plural, do we just add “s”? Student: No, because it ends in y. Tutor: Good. What letter comes before the y? Student: r. Tutor: Right, and that is a consonant. So what happens to the y? Student: It changes to i and we add es. Tutor: Exactly. So “secretary” becomes secretaries.

questions.questionText: Rewrite the sentence giving one word for the underlined group of words: Eating too much sugar can lead to health problems.
questions.instruction: In each of the questions 24 to 25, give the plural of the given word.

parts.answer: cause
acceptable_answers: bring about
result in (but only one word is required)
parts.explanation: Tutor: The underlined words are “lead to.” We need one word with the same meaning. What happens when too much sugar leads to health problems? Student: It causes them. Tutor: Correct. “Cause” means bring about or make something happen. Tutor: So the sentence can be rewritten as: “Eating too much sugar can cause health problems.” Tutor: Because the instruction says one word, “cause” is the best choice.

questions.questionText: Rewrite the sentence giving one word for the underlined group of words: My mother visits the dentist two times a year.
questions.instruction: In each of the questions 26 to 27, rewrite the sentence giving one word for the underlined group of words.

parts.answer: twice
parts.explanation: Tutor: We need one word to replace “two times.” What one word means that? Student: Twice. Tutor: Correct. So the sentence becomes: “My mother visits the dentist twice a year.” Tutor: This is a common exam word, so always remember: - one time = once - two times = twice

questions.questionText: Rewrite the sentence giving one word for the underlined group of words: When Mr. Byaruhanga entered the restaurant, the waitress gave him a list of food available.
questions.instruction: In each of the questions 26 to 27, rewrite the sentence giving one word for the underlined group of words.

parts.answer: menu
acceptable_answers: bill (wrong)
order list (not best)
parts.explanation: Tutor: What do you call the list showing food available in a restaurant? Student: Menu. Tutor: Correct. A menu tells customers what food and drinks are available. Tutor: So the sentence becomes: “When Mr. Byaruhanga entered the restaurant, the waitress gave him a menu.” Tutor: Be careful not to confuse “menu” with “bill.” A bill is what you pay after eating.

questions.questionText: Use the word air in a sentence to show that you know the difference in its meaning.
questions.instruction: In each of the questions 28 to 30, use the word in a sentence to show that you know the difference in its meaning.

parts.answer: We need fresh air to breathe.
acceptable_answers: The room was full of hot air.
Birds fly in the air.
parts.explanation: Tutor: This question is testing your ability to use the word correctly in a meaningful sentence. What does “air” mean here? Student: The gas we breathe. Tutor: Good. Can you make a clear sentence with that meaning? Student: We need fresh air to breathe. Tutor: Excellent. That is short, correct, and meaningful. Tutor: In this kind of question, avoid very short fragments. Write a full sentence.

questions.questionText: Use the word heir in a sentence to show that you know the difference in its meaning.
questions.instruction: In each of the questions 28 to 30, use the word in a sentence to show that you know the difference in its meaning.

parts.answer: The king’s son is the heir to the throne.
acceptable_answers: She is the heir to her father’s property.
The heir received the land.
parts.explanation: Tutor: The word “heir” sounds like “air,” but it has a different meaning. What does “heir” mean? Student: A person who inherits something. Tutor: Correct. Often it means someone who receives property, wealth, or a position after another person. Tutor: Can you make a sentence? Student: The king’s son is the heir to the throne. Tutor: Very good. That clearly shows you understand the meaning. Tutor: This kind of question is about homophones—words that sound alike but have different meanings.

questions.questionText: Turkeys are bigger than cocks. (Rewrite the sentence using: ... as ... as ...)
questions.instruction: Rewrite the sentence using: …as……..as…

parts.answer: Cocks are not as big as turkeys.
acceptable_answers: A cock is not as big as a turkey.
parts.explanation: Tutor: Let’s first understand the original sentence. Who is bigger? Student: Turkeys are bigger than cocks. Tutor: Correct. Now we must use the pattern as…as. When two things are not equal, what do we usually add? Student: “Not as…as” Tutor: Exactly. So instead of saying “Turkeys are bigger than cocks,” we turn it around and say the smaller one is not as big as the larger one. Tutor: Which is the smaller one here? Student: Cocks. Tutor: Good. So the answer becomes? Student: Cocks are not as big as turkeys. Tutor: Perfect. Notice that after as, we use the adjective in its basic form: big, not bigger.

questions.questionText: The bride sent us a cake. Her wedding took place last weekend. (Rewrite as one sentence using: whose)
questions.instruction: Rewrite as one sentence using: whose

parts.instruction: Rewrite as one sentence using: whose
parts.answer: The bride whose wedding took place last weekend sent us a cake.
acceptable_answers: The bride
whose wedding took place last weekend
sent us a cake.
parts.explanation: Tutor: We have two sentences about the same person. Who is that person? Student: The bride. Tutor: Correct. One sentence tells us what she did. The other gives extra information about her wedding. Tutor: Which joining word in the instruction helps us show possession or relationship? Student: Whose. Tutor: Exactly. “Whose” connects the bride to the wedding. Tutor: So how do we begin? Student: The bride whose wedding took place last weekend… Tutor: Good. Then finish with the main action. Student: …sent us a cake. Tutor: Excellent. That gives one clear sentence.

questions.questionText: No sooner had the rain begun than the lesson stopped. (Rewrite the sentence using: immediately)
questions.instruction: Rewrite the sentence using: immediately

parts.answer: Immediately the rain began, the lesson stopped.
acceptable_answers: The lesson stopped immediately the rain began.
parts.explanation: Tutor: The expression no sooner…than… shows what kind of relationship? Student: That one thing happened at once after the other. Tutor: Exactly. We are asked to use immediately, which also shows quick sequence. Tutor: Which event happened first? Student: The rain began. Tutor: And what happened right after? Student: The lesson stopped. Tutor: Good. So we can rewrite it as: Immediately the rain began, the lesson stopped. Tutor: The key meaning is “at once” or “without delay.”

questions.questionText: Aunt Sandra made a shopping list before we went to the market. (Rewrite the sentence using: ... after ...)
questions.instruction: Rewrite the sentence using: …after………..

parts.answer: After Aunt Sandra had made a shopping list, we went to the market.
acceptable_answers: We went to the market after Aunt Sandra had made a shopping list.
parts.explanation: Tutor: The original sentence uses before. Which event happened first? Student: Aunt Sandra made the shopping list. Tutor: Correct. Which event happened second? Student: We went to the market. Tutor: Good. To use after, we must start with the later event or show clearly that one action was completed first. Tutor: So what sentence works well? Student: After Aunt Sandra had made a shopping list, we went to the market. Tutor: Exactly. Using had made helps show that the list was completed before the trip.

questions.questionText: The children preferred swimming to jogging. (Rewrite the sentence using: ... than ...)
questions.instruction: Rewrite the sentence using: ……………than…………….

parts.answer: The children liked swimming more than jogging.
acceptable_answers: The children preferred swimming rather than jogging. (does not fit the exact cue as well)
parts.explanation: Tutor: What does preferred swimming to jogging mean? Student: They liked swimming more. Tutor: Exactly. Since the instruction gives than, we need a comparative structure. Tutor: Which word helps us compare the two activities? Student: More. Tutor: Correct. So we say: The children liked swimming more than jogging. Tutor: The meaning stays the same: swimming was their first choice.

questions.questionText: It is our responsibility to keep our classrooms tidy. (Rewrite the sentence using: ... responsible ...)
questions.instruction: Rewrite the sentence using: ………….responsible…………….

parts.answer: We are responsible for keeping our classrooms tidy.
acceptable_answers: It is we who are responsible for keeping our classrooms tidy. (less direct)
parts.explanation: Tutor: The word given is responsible. The original sentence says: “It is our responsibility…” What noun do you see there? Student: Responsibility. Tutor: Good. We need to change that noun into the adjective responsible. Tutor: Who is responsible? Student: We are. Tutor: Exactly. Responsible for doing what? Student: Keeping our classrooms tidy. Tutor: Correct. So the best answer is: We are responsible for keeping our classrooms tidy.

questions.questionText: Nicholas was washing the car. His sister was preparing breakfast. (Rewrite as one sentence beginning: While ...)
questions.instruction: Rewrite as one sentence beginning: While…

parts.instruction: Rewrite as one sentence beginning: While…
parts.answer: While Nicholas was washing the car, his sister was preparing breakfast.
acceptable_answers: While his sister was preparing breakfast
Nicholas was washing the car.
parts.explanation: Tutor: These two actions happened at the same time. Which joining word shows that? Student: While. Tutor: Correct. The instruction says begin with While, so which action will come right after it? Student: Nicholas was washing the car. Tutor: Good. Then we add the second action. Student: …his sister was preparing breakfast. Tutor: Exactly. This shows both actions were happening at the same time.

questions.questionText: Mbowa will not travel by bus. Mbowa will not travel by taxi. (Rewrite as one sentence using: ... neither ... nor ...)
questions.instruction: Rewrite as one sentence using: ………….. neither ……….. nor …………..

parts.instruction: Rewrite as one sentence using: ………….. neither ……….. nor …………..
parts.answer: Mbowa will travel by neither bus nor taxi.
acceptable_answers: Mbowa will neither travel by bus nor by taxi.
parts.explanation: Tutor: We have two negative ideas. What special pair joins two negatives neatly? Student: Neither…nor. Tutor: Correct. The meaning is that both choices are rejected. Tutor: So instead of repeating “will not” twice, we bring the two travel options together. Tutor: What sentence do we get? Student: Mbowa will travel by neither bus nor taxi. Tutor: Good. Another correct form is: Mbowa will neither travel by bus nor by taxi. Tutor: Both carry the same meaning.

questions.questionText: The teacher asked Nambuya why she had missed lessons the previous day. (Rewrite the sentence ending: ... ?" the teacher asked Nambuya.)
questions.instruction: Rewrite the sentence ending: ……………..?” the teacher asked Nambuya.)

parts.instruction: Rewrite the sentence ending: ……………..?” the teacher asked Nambuya.)
parts.answer: “Why did you miss lessons yesterday?” the teacher asked Nambuya.
acceptable_answers: “Why did you miss class yesterday?” the teacher asked Nambuya. (if “lessons” is changed carefully
but keep original wording where possible)
parts.explanation: Tutor: The original sentence is in reported speech. We need to change it back into direct speech. Tutor: In reported speech, “the previous day” changes to what in direct speech? Student: Yesterday. Tutor: Correct. Also, “she had missed” becomes what when the teacher is speaking directly to Nambuya? Student: You missed. Tutor: Exactly. Since this is a question, we write it with quotation marks and a question mark. Tutor: So the final answer is: “Why did you miss lessons yesterday?” the teacher asked Nambuya.

questions.questionText: The tailor used a pair of scissors to cut the cloth. (Rewrite the sentence beginning: The tailor cut ...)
questions.instruction: Rewrite the sentence beginning: The tailor cut……………….

parts.instruction: Rewrite the sentence beginning: The tailor cut……………….
parts.answer: The tailor cut the cloth with a pair of scissors.
acceptable_answers: The tailor cut the cloth using a pair of scissors.
parts.explanation: Tutor: The instruction says begin with The tailor cut… So which part must come first now? Student: The tailor cut the cloth… Tutor: Correct. Then how do we show the tool that was used? Student: With a pair of scissors. Tutor: Exactly. So the sentence becomes: The tailor cut the cloth with a pair of scissors. Tutor: The meaning stays the same. Only the structure changes.

questions.questionText: Shakirah did not post the letters. (Rewrite the sentence ending: ... by Shakirah.)
questions.instruction: Rewrite the sentence ending: …………….. by Shakirah.

parts.instruction: Rewrite the sentence ending: …………….. by Shakirah.
parts.answer: The letters were not posted by Shakirah.
acceptable_answers: The letters were never posted by Shakirah. (adds meaning not in original)
parts.explanation: Tutor: This question wants a change of voice. The original sentence is in the active voice. Who is doing the action? Student: Shakirah. Tutor: Correct. What is receiving the action? Student: The letters. Tutor: Good. In the passive voice, what moves to the beginning? Student: The letters. Tutor: Exactly. Since the original is negative, we must keep the negative form. Tutor: So the answer is: The letters were not posted by Shakirah.

questions.questionText: I have not been to the market for two months. (Rewrite the sentence ending: ... ago.)
questions.instruction: Rewrite the sentence ending: …………….. ago.

parts.answer: I last went to the market two months ago.
acceptable_answers: The last time I went to the market was two months ago.
parts.explanation: Tutor: The phrase for two months tells us the length of time. But the new sentence must end with ago, which points to a past time. Tutor: So instead of saying how long you have not gone, what do we say? Student: The last time I went. Tutor: Exactly. What is the correct sentence? Student: I last went to the market two months ago. Tutor: Perfect. That keeps the meaning and fits the instruction.

questions.questionText: Our players were very tired. They could not walk back to school. (Rewrite as one sentence using: ... too ... to ...)
questions.instruction: Rewrite as one sentence using: …………..too………….to…………..

parts.answer: Our players were too tired to walk back to school.
parts.explanation: Tutor: This is the too…to structure again. What does it show? Student: That something is excessive and prevents another action. Tutor: Correct. Here, what was excessive? Student: They were very tired. Tutor: And what could they not do? Student: Walk back to school. Tutor: So we join both ideas into one sentence: Our players were too tired to walk back to school.

questions.questionText: Kapere is a very rich man. Kapere does not have a car. (Rewrite as one sentence beginning: Although ...)
questions.instruction: Rewrite as one sentence beginning: Although…………….

parts.instruction: Rewrite as one sentence beginning: Although…………….
parts.answer: Although Kapere is a very rich man, he does not have a car.
acceptable_answers: Although Kapere is very rich
he does not have a car.
parts.explanation: Tutor: The word although shows contrast. What two ideas are being contrasted? Student: He is rich, but he has no car. Tutor: Exactly. So we begin with the first idea after although. Tutor: Then we add the surprising second idea. Student: Although Kapere is a very rich man, he does not have a car. Tutor: Correct. That is a clear contrast sentence.

questions.questionText: Mrs. Odeke paid fifteen thousand shillings for a kilo of meat. (Rewrite the sentence using: ... spent ...)
questions.instruction: Rewrite the sentence using: ……………..spent………………

parts.answer: Mrs. Odeke spent fifteen thousand shillings on a kilo of meat.
acceptable_answers: Mrs. Odeke spent fifteen thousand shillings buying a kilo of meat. (less direct)
parts.explanation: Tutor: The instruction gives the word spent. In the original sentence, what did Mrs. Odeke do? Student: She paid fifteen thousand shillings. Tutor: Correct. We can express the same idea using spent … on … Tutor: So what did she spend? Student: Fifteen thousand shillings. Tutor: On what? Student: A kilo of meat. Tutor: Good. So the answer is: Mrs. Odeke spent fifteen thousand shillings on a kilo of meat.

questions.questionText: She hooted at the cyclist so as to save his life. (Rewrite the sentence beginning: In order to ...)
questions.instruction: Rewrite the sentence beginning: In order to …………………..

parts.instruction: Rewrite the sentence beginning: In order to …………………..
parts.answer: In order to save his life, she hooted at the cyclist.
acceptable_answers: In order to save the cyclist’s life
she hooted at him.
parts.explanation: Tutor: The phrase so as to and in order to usually mean the same thing. What was the purpose of hooting? Student: To save his life. Tutor: Correct. Since the instruction says begin with In order to, that purpose comes first. Tutor: Then what action follows? Student: She hooted at the cyclist. Tutor: Exactly. So the sentence becomes: In order to save his life, she hooted at the cyclist.

questions.questionText: You must tuck in your shirt or else you won’t look smart. (Rewrite the sentence beginning: If ...)
questions.instruction: Rewrite the sentence beginning: If ………………….

parts.answer: If you do not tuck in your shirt, you will not look smart.
acceptable_answers: If you fail to tuck in your shirt
you will not look smart.
parts.explanation: Tutor: The phrase or else can often be changed into an if sentence. What condition is being given? Student: Tuck in your shirt. Tutor: And what happens if you do not? Student: You won’t look smart. Tutor: Exactly. So we rewrite it as: If you do not tuck in your shirt, you will not look smart. Tutor: We keep the warning, but change the structure.

questions.questionText: Aida did not receive any money from her guardian, did she? (Rewrite the sentence ending: ... didn't she?)
questions.instruction: Rewrite the sentence ending: …………….. didn’t she?

parts.instruction: Rewrite the sentence ending: …………….. didn’t she?
parts.answer: Aida received no money from her guardian, didn’t she?
acceptable_answers: Aida received no money from her guardian
did she? (wrong tag for positive statement)
parts.explanation: Tutor: This is a question tag change. The original statement is negative: did not receive. The tag is positive: did she? Tutor: But now the ending given is didn’t she? That means the main statement must become what? Student: Positive. Tutor: Correct. How can we keep the same meaning but make the verb positive? Student: Use “received no money.” Tutor: Exactly. That makes the statement positive in grammar form but still negative in meaning. Tutor: So the correct sentence is: Aida received no money from her guardian, didn’t she?

questions.questionText: A radio is a useful source of information and so is a television. (Rewrite the sentence beginning: Both ...)
questions.instruction: Rewrite the sentence beginning: Both ………………..)

parts.answer: Both a radio and a television are useful sources of information.
acceptable_answers: Both radio and television are useful sources of information.
parts.explanation: Tutor: The word both helps us talk about two things together. What are the two things here? Student: A radio and a television. Tutor: Correct. And what is true about both of them? Student: They are useful sources of information. Tutor: Exactly. So we combine them into one sentence: Both a radio and a television are useful sources of information. Tutor: Notice that the verb becomes are because the subject is plural.

questions.questionText: The boy who won the race is our relative. (Rewrite the sentence ending: ... to us.)
questions.instruction: Rewrite the sentence ending: …………….. to us.)

parts.instruction: Rewrite the sentence ending: …………….. to us.)
parts.answer: The boy who won the race is related to us.
acceptable_answers: The boy that won the race is related to us.
parts.explanation: Tutor: We are told to end with to us. Which word from the original sentence can change into a related form? Student: Relative? Tutor: Correct. “Relative” is a noun, but here we need the adjective form related. Tutor: So instead of saying “is our relative,” what do we say? Student: Is related to us. Tutor: Exactly. So the final sentence is: The boy who won the race is related to us.

questions.questionText: Read the passage below and then answer, in full sentences, the questions that follow.
questions.instruction: Answer in full sentences.

sharedContext.content:
Lolo and Jemba were two great friends. They went to Mushanga Primary School. In this school, lunch was only for those whose parents had contributed towards their feeding.

One Friday afternoon, Jemba got so hungry that his stomach began making continuous long deep sounds. He hadn’t carried anything to eat while at school that day. Lolo heard the sounds that came from his friend’s stomach and sympathized with him.

“Let’s go to the trading centre and buy something to eat,” he said with a smile. “I have got some money.”

“Wow!” Jemba said. “I’ll give you a lift on my bicycle.”

Unlike Jemba’s parents, the parents of Lolo, once in a while, at least gave their son some money. With this money, he could buy pancakes, buns and some juice to have as lunch. He often shared this so-called lunch with his best friend.

With Lolo sitting on the carrier, Jemba got on his bicycle and rode off. The speed at which he rode was very high. He was very excited. Besides, he wanted to get back to school in time for the afternoon lessons. It was a busy road and, as Jemba sped up, he kept ringing the bicycle bell. Unfortunately, he lost his balance and they both fell off the bicycle. Lolo suffered a leg injury while Jemba had a fractured hand.

They were lucky that some passers-by rushed them to a nearby clinic. They then rang their head teacher who immediately arrived at the clinic. She thanked the nurses for treating the boys. She then informed the boys’ parents about the accident.

Three weeks later, after Lolo and Jemba had got better, they returned to school. The whole school was so pleased to see them. The head teacher, however, warned all pupils against leaving school without permission. She also held a meeting with all parents to discuss the issue of school meals. Today, all pupils have lunch at school. It is not surprising that their academic performance has greatly improved.

parts.prompt: To which school did the two great friends go?
parts.answer: The two friends went to Mushanga Primary School.
acceptable_answers: They studied at Mushanga Primary School.
parts.explanation: Tutor: For this question, do we need to guess? Student: No. Tutor: Correct. The answer is directly in the first lines of the passage. What is the name of the school? Student: Mushanga Primary School. Tutor: Good. Now remember: the instruction says answer in full sentences. So do not write only the school name. Student: I should say, “The two friends went to Mushanga Primary School.” Tutor: Exactly.

parts.prompt: Why wasn’t there lunch for Lolo and Jemba in this school?
parts.answer: There was no lunch because parents had to contribute towards their children’s feeding.
acceptable_answers: Lunch was not provided by the school
only pupils whose parents contributed food could get lunch.
parts.explanation: Tutor: This is a “why” question. That means we must give the reason. What does the passage tell us about lunch? Student: Parents had to contribute towards feeding. Tutor: Correct. So the school did not provide lunch for everybody freely. Tutor: How do we turn that into a full answer? Student: There was no lunch because parents had to contribute towards their children’s feeding. Tutor: Very good. Always answer the reason clearly.

parts.prompt: What could Lolo do with the money that his parents sometimes gave him?
parts.answer: Lolo could buy pancakes and buns and some juice for lunch.
acceptable_answers: He could buy lunch with it
he could buy something to eat.
parts.explanation: Tutor: Here we must be careful. The passage gives specific examples. What did Lolo buy with the money? Student: Pancakes, buns and some juice. Tutor: Correct. Since the question asks “what could he do,” we can answer by saying he could buy lunch. Tutor: A stronger answer uses the examples from the passage. Student: Lolo could buy pancakes, buns and some juice for lunch. Tutor: Excellent.

parts.prompt: Why did Jemba ride at a very high speed?
parts.answer: Jemba rode at a very high speed because he was excited and wanted to enjoy the ride and still get back to school in time for the afternoon lessons.
acceptable_answers: He was excited
he wanted to enjoy the ride
he also wanted to return to school in time.
parts.explanation: Tutor: This answer has more than one reason. What are they? Student: He was excited. Tutor: Good. What else? Student: He wanted to enjoy the ride. Tutor: Correct. And one more? Student: He wanted to get back to school in time. Tutor: Excellent. Since all three ideas are in the passage, a full answer can combine them. Tutor: This is stronger than giving only one reason.

parts.prompt: Why did Jemba ring the bicycle bell as he sped up?
parts.answer: Jemba rang the bicycle bell to warn the old man who was in front of him.
acceptable_answers: He wanted the old man to move out of the way
he rang it because there was an old man ahead of him.
parts.explanation: Tutor: Let’s follow the events closely. Who was in front of him? Student: An old man. Tutor: Yes. So why ring the bell? Student: To warn him. Tutor: Correct. Good comprehension answers often follow normal real-life logic and the events in the passage.

parts.prompt: What led to the accident, according to the passage?
parts.answer: The accident happened because Jemba lost his balance and both he and the old man fell off the bicycle.
acceptable_answers: Jemba rode too fast and lost balance
he knocked the old man after losing balance.
parts.explanation: Tutor: This question asks what directly led to the accident. Was it only speed? Student: He also lost balance. Tutor: Exactly. Speed explains the danger, but the immediate cause in the passage is that he lost his balance. Tutor: That is the detail the learner should pick.

parts.prompt: What did the head teacher do immediately she arrived at the clinic?
parts.answer: Immediately she arrived at the clinic, the head teacher thanked the nurses for treating the boys.
acceptable_answers: She thanked the nurses first.
parts.explanation: Tutor: The word “immediately” is important. We are looking for the first thing she did. What came first in the passage? Student: She thanked the nurses. Tutor: Correct. What came after that? Student: She informed the parents. Tutor: Good. So the correct answer is the first action only.

parts.prompt: For how long did the two friends stay away from school?
parts.answer: The two friends stayed away from school for three weeks.
acceptable_answers: They were away from school for three weeks.
parts.explanation: Tutor: This is a direct fact question. We only need the time period. What was it? Student: Three weeks. Tutor: Good. Put it into a full sentence.

parts.prompt: Why did the academic performance of the pupils in this school greatly improve?
parts.answer: The academic performance greatly improved because all pupils started having lunch at school.
acceptable_answers: It improved after the school started providing lunch
pupils could study better because they had lunch.
parts.explanation: Tutor: We must connect the last part of the passage. What change happened in the school? Student: All pupils started having lunch. Tutor: Yes. And the result? Student: Their academic performance improved. Tutor: Good. That shows cause and effect.

parts.prompt: Suggest a suitable title to the passage.
parts.answer: Lunch at School
acceptable_answers: The Importance of School Lunch
Lolo and Jemba
A Bicycle Accident
How Lunch Improved Performance
parts.explanation: Tutor: A title must be short and capture the main idea. What is the passage mostly about? Student: Lunch and what happened because they didn’t have it. Tutor: Good. A bicycle accident is part of the story, but what is the bigger message at the end? Student: That lunch at school matters. Tutor: Exactly. So “Lunch at School” is a strong title. Tutor: In title questions, more than one answer may be accepted if it fits the central idea.

questions.questionText: Read the poem below and then answer, in full sentences, the questions that follow.
questions.instruction: Answer in full sentences.

sharedContext.content:
But a terrible animal as well
Sleepless nights I spend
As I try to prepare for you.
Before I meet you,
I’m briefed like a bride and a bridegroom
So as not to fear you
But to defeat you and get a good grade.
On your special day that I face you,
I’m surrounded by strange men and women
They call them invigilators
Giving out and collecting papers, their duty.
When your results are out
And success is on my side,
I rejoice
Forgotten are the sleepless nights.

parts.prompt: What is the poem about?
parts.answer: The poem is about examination.
acceptable_answers: It is about exams
it is about preparing for examinations.
parts.explanation: Tutor: The poem begins with the line “Examination, a friend…” What is the main thing being addressed throughout the poem? Student: Examination. Tutor: Correct. So the simplest and strongest answer is direct.

parts.prompt: Why does the speaker spend sleepless nights?
parts.answer: The speaker spends sleepless nights because he or she is preparing for examination.
acceptable_answers: Because the speaker wants to prepare well
because the speaker wants to defeat examination and get a good grade.
parts.explanation: Tutor: Does the poem say the speaker is sleepless for no reason? Student: No. Tutor: What reason is given? Student: To prepare for examination. Tutor: Exactly. Always answer from the poem’s own words when possible.

parts.prompt: What happens before the speaker meets examination?
parts.answer: Before meeting examination, the speaker is briefed like a bride and a bridegroom.
acceptable_answers: The speaker is briefed before meeting examination.
parts.explanation: Tutor: We need the event that comes before meeting examination. Which line tells us that? Student: “Before I meet you, I’m briefed like a bride and a bridegroom.” Tutor: Correct. That is the evidence line.

parts.prompt: Why should the speaker defeat examination?
parts.answer: The speaker should defeat examination in order to get a good grade.
acceptable_answers: To pass well
to succeed.
parts.explanation: Tutor: The poem gives the reason directly. What does the speaker want after defeating examination? Student: A good grade. Tutor: Exactly.

parts.prompt: Why does the speaker refer to invigilators as strange men and women?
parts.answer: The speaker calls them strange men and women because they are unfamiliar people who surround learners during examination.
acceptable_answers: Because the speaker does not know them
because they appear as outsiders during the examination.
parts.explanation: Tutor: Are invigilators usually the learner’s close friends? Student: No. Tutor: So why describe them as “strange”? Student: Because they are unfamiliar. Tutor: Correct. The poem is expressing the tense feeling of the exam room.

parts.prompt: What is the duty of the strange men and women?
parts.answer: Their duty is giving out and collecting papers.
acceptable_answers: They supervise the examination and give out and collect papers.
parts.explanation: Tutor: The poem answers this directly. What do the invigilators do? Student: They give out and collect papers. Tutor: Exactly. That is the expected answer.

parts.prompt: When does the speaker rejoice?
parts.answer: The speaker rejoices when the results are out and success is on his or her side.
acceptable_answers: When the results come out
when the speaker succeeds.
parts.explanation: Tutor: We need the exact condition for rejoicing. Does the speaker rejoice during preparation? Student: No. Tutor: When then? Student: When the results are out and success is on the speaker’s side. Tutor: Correct.

parts.prompt: What does the speaker forget?
parts.answer: The speaker forgets the sleepless nights.
acceptable_answers: The hard preparation
the nights spent preparing.
parts.explanation: Tutor: At the end of the poem, what is forgotten? Student: The sleepless nights. Tutor: Yes. Success makes the earlier suffering feel worth it.

parts.prompt: Give another word or group of words with the same meaning as terrible.
parts.answer: awful
acceptable_answers: bad
dreadful
frightening
parts.explanation: Tutor: We are not being asked to copy from the poem. We need a synonym. What word is close in meaning to “terrible”? Student: Awful. Tutor: Correct. Other answers may also work if they truly carry the same meaning.

parts.prompt: Give another word or group of words with the same meaning as duty.
parts.answer: responsibility
acceptable_answers: job
task
work
parts.explanation: Tutor: What is a duty? Student: Something a person is supposed to do. Tutor: Exactly. A strong synonym is responsibility.

questions.questionText: Study the record carefully and then answer, in full sentences, the questions that follow.
questions.instruction: Answer in full sentences.

sharedContext.intro: Below is a record kept by a P.7 class monitor at Divine Junior School, Nalusaga. It shows the pupils in charge of cleaning the P.7 classroom each morning for one week, their supervisors, and the comments made each day. Study it carefully and then answer, in full sentences, the questions that follow.
sharedContext.heading: WEEK FIVE, TERM TWO (2024)
sharedContext.content_markdown:
| Day | Pupils Responsible | Supervisor | Comment |
|---|---|---|---|
| Monday | Mulumba Felix, Asio Mariam, Maiso Toni | Acen Lisa | Work well done! |
| Tuesday | Oketcho James, Mangara Richard, Auma Dora | Tamale Fred | Work incomplete. Part of the classroom not cleaned. |
| Wednesday | Mafabi Leo, Apio Liz, Oyo Joseph | Nassanga Esther | Excellent work done! |
| Thursday | Sidia Sania, Akiasiina Noet, Bwambale Tito | Musoke Edwin | Classroom cleaned at break time. |
| Friday | Candiru Joan, Chelimo Aziz, Abalo Phiona | Edema Philip | Cleaning not done. Zonal ball games. |

parts.prompt: Who kept the above record?
parts.answer: The record was kept by a P.7 class monitor.
acceptable_answers: A Primary Seven class monitor kept the record.
parts.explanation: Tutor: This answer is not in the table body only. Where do we find it? Student: In the introduction above the table. Tutor: Exactly. That is why learners must read everything, not just the table lines.

parts.prompt: When was the above record taken?
parts.answer: The record was taken in Week Five, Term Two, 2024.
acceptable_answers: It was taken during Week Five of Term Two
parts.explanation: Tutor: The table heading gives the time. What does it say? Student: Week Five, Term Two, 2024. Tutor: Correct. That heading is the answer source.

parts.prompt: How many pupils were in charge of cleaning the classroom each day?
parts.answer: Three pupils were in charge of cleaning the classroom each day.
acceptable_answers: Each day had three pupils responsible.
parts.explanation: Tutor: Look at each day. How many names appear under “Pupils Responsible”? Student: Three names. Tutor: Good. Always count carefully and check that the pattern is the same for all days.

parts.prompt: Why was work considered incomplete on Tuesday?
parts.answer: Work was considered incomplete on Tuesday because part of the classroom was not cleaned.
acceptable_answers: Because some part of the classroom remained dirty.
parts.explanation: Tutor: Which column gives the reason? Student: The comment column. Tutor: Correct. What exactly does it say for Tuesday? Student: Part of the classroom not cleaned. Tutor: Good. Turn that into a full sentence.

parts.prompt: How many people supervised the cleaning of the classroom that week?
parts.answer: Five people supervised the cleaning that week.
acceptable_answers: There were five supervisors.
parts.explanation: Tutor: Now be careful. We are not counting days. We are counting supervisors. Does any supervisor appear more than once? Student: No, each day has a different name. Tutor: Right. Since there are five days, there are five supervisors.

parts.prompt: On which day was excellent work done?
parts.answer: Excellent work was done on Wednesday.
acceptable_answers: On Wednesday
excellent work was done.
parts.explanation: Tutor: Look through the comment column. Which day has the words “Excellent work done!”? Student: Wednesday. Tutor: Correct.

parts.prompt: Which pupils cleaned the classroom at break time?
parts.answer: Sidia Sania, Akiasiina Noet and Bwambale Tito cleaned the classroom at break time.
acceptable_answers: The pupils on Thursday cleaned at break time.
parts.explanation: Tutor: The clue is in the Thursday comment: “Classroom cleaned at break time.” Which pupils were on duty that day? Student: Akiasiina Noet and Bwambale Tito… and also Sidia Sania. Tutor: Good catch. Since three pupils were listed for Thursday, all those pupils were responsible. The learner should answer with all the names given for that day.

parts.prompt: Why wasn’t the classroom cleaned on Friday?
parts.answer: The classroom was not cleaned on Friday because there were zonal ball games.
acceptable_answers: Because the pupils had zonal ball games.
parts.explanation: Tutor: Which column helps us here again? Student: The comment column. Tutor: Correct. What comment appears for Friday? Student: Cleaning not done. Zonal ball games. Tutor: Good. So the games explain why cleaning was not done.

parts.prompt: What was the role of Acen Lisa, according to the record above?
parts.answer: Acen Lisa was the supervisor on Monday.
acceptable_answers: She supervised the classroom cleaning on Monday.
parts.explanation: Tutor: Find Acen Lisa in the table. Which column is her name under? Student: Supervisor. Tutor: Exactly. That tells us her role.

parts.prompt: Why would you encourage your class monitor to keep such a record?
parts.answer: I would encourage my class monitor to keep such a record because it helps to monitor work, identify mistakes, and improve cleanliness.
acceptable_answers: It promotes responsibility
it helps teachers supervise
it keeps evidence of work done.
parts.explanation: Tutor: This is one of the few questions where the learner must think beyond the table. So we need a reasonable answer, not a copied one. Tutor: Why is record-keeping useful? Student: It helps people know what was done. Tutor: Correct. It also helps follow up mistakes and improve responsibility. Tutor: Since this is open-ended, more than one good answer may be accepted if it is sensible and connected to the record.

questions.questionText: Pictures A–F tell a story. Study each picture carefully and then write one sentence to describe what is happening.
questions.instruction: Use the guide words where helpful. Answer in full sentences where applicable.

sharedContext.intro: Pictures A – F tell a story. Study each picture carefully and then write one sentence to describe what is happening.

parts.prompt: Write one sentence to describe what is happening.
parts.answer: Two girls are walking along the road.
acceptable_answers: Two girls are moving together on the roadside
two girls are going somewhere on foot.
parts.explanation: Tutor: At Picture A, do we already see danger happening? Student: Not yet. Tutor: Correct. We only describe what is clearly happening. Never jump too far ahead in the first picture.

parts.prompt: Write one sentence to describe what is happening.
parts.answer: Two men are forcing the girls into a car.
acceptable_answers: The girls are being abducted into a vehicle
the men are kidnapping the girls.
parts.explanation: Tutor: What makes this picture serious? Student: The girls are being forced into the car. Tutor: Exactly. Words like forcing, abducting, or kidnapping fit well.

parts.prompt: Write one sentence to describe what is happening.
parts.answer: The girls are jumping out of the car as it stops.
acceptable_answers: The girls are escaping from the vehicle
the girls are leaving the stopped car.
parts.explanation: Tutor: At this stage, we must follow the picture carefully. What do we actually see? Student: Someone is getting out quickly. Tutor: Good. So the idea is escape or getting out as the car has stopped.

parts.prompt: Write one sentence to describe what is happening.
parts.answer: The girls are reporting the matter to police officers at the station.
acceptable_answers: The girls have reached the police station
the police are listening to the girls’ report.
parts.explanation: Tutor: Which clue in the drawing makes this answer clear? Student: The words “Police Station.” Tutor: Exactly. Always use visible clues in picture questions.

parts.prompt: Write one sentence to describe what is happening.
parts.answer: The police have stopped the vehicle at a roadblock.
acceptable_answers: Police officers are checking the car at a police check point
the car has been stopped by police.
parts.explanation: Tutor: The sign in the picture says “Police Check.” What does that suggest? Student: A roadblock or checkpoint. Tutor: Correct. So the sentence should mention the car being stopped.

parts.prompt: Write one sentence to describe what is happening.
parts.answer: The police officers are arresting the two men as the girls hug happily.
acceptable_answers: The kidnappers are being arrested
the girls are happy because the men have been arrested.
parts.explanation: Tutor: This picture shows the outcome. What two things happen at once? Student: The men are arrested and the girls are happy. Tutor: Exactly. A strong sentence can combine both if it remains clear.

parts.prompt: What shows that the girls are happy in Picture F?
parts.answer: The girls are hugging each other, which shows they are happy.
acceptable_answers: Their body language shows happiness
they look excited and relieved.
parts.explanation: Tutor: We must answer using evidence from the picture. What can we actually see? Student: They are hugging. Tutor: Good. That visible action is your evidence.

parts.prompt: What is likely to happen to the two men after Picture F?
parts.answer: The two men are likely to be taken to court and punished.
acceptable_answers: They may be imprisoned
they may be charged by police.
parts.explanation: Tutor: This is not directly shown, so we infer. Since they were arrested, what usually follows? Student: They go to court or prison. Tutor: Correct. This is an inference question, so a sensible answer is accepted.

parts.prompt: What lesson does this story teach you?
parts.answer: The story teaches us to report crimes quickly to the police.
acceptable_answers: Crime does not pay
children should be careful with strangers
police help protect people.
parts.explanation: Tutor: Lesson questions are open-ended, but the answer must come from the story. What main lesson do you see? Student: That crimes should be reported. Tutor: Good. Other lessons may also work if they fit the events.

parts.prompt: Suggest a suitable title for the story.
parts.answer: The Abduction of Two Girls
acceptable_answers: The Kidnappers Were Arrested
Saved by the Police
The Police Checkpoint
parts.explanation: Tutor: A good title must be short and capture the main action. What is the central event here? Student: The girls were abducted. Tutor: Correct. That makes The Abduction of Two Girls a strong title.

questions.questionText: Composition / Informal Letter

sharedContext.content:
Your school organised an educational tour to one of the national game parks in Uganda. Each pupil was asked to pay for it. Your uncle, who lives in Nairobi, Kenya, paid the money that was required. Write a letter to thank him. In your letter, give him more Information about the game park you visited and tell him what you leant while there.
Use MUNA PRIMARY SCHOOL, PO BOX 44, MOLO as your address

parts.prompt:
Your school organised an educational tour to one of the national game parks in Uganda. Each pupil was asked to pay for it. Your uncle, who lives in Nairobi, Kenya, paid the money that was required. Write a letter to thank him. In your letter, give him more Information about the game park you visited and tell him what you leant while there.
Use MUNA PRIMARY SCHOOL, PO BOX 44, MOLO as your address

ambiguities.issue: The answers document refers to an old man ahead of Jemba and says both Jemba and the old man fell off the bicycle, but the official PDF passage on page 8 does not mention an old man. The PDF says Jemba rang the bell as he sped up, then lost his balance and 'they both fell off the bicycle,' which reads as Jemba and Lolo.
ambiguities.current_json_handling: Preserved the DOCX picked answers and tutor explanations for these parts because the answers document is the requested source of truth for answers/explanations. Review before insert.

ambiguities.issue: The answers document includes both a picked answer with only two names and a later 'Better Picked Answer' with all three Thursday pupils.
ambiguities.current_json_handling: Used the Better Picked Answer with all three names.

ambiguities.issue: The provided answers document does not include a model answer, acceptable answers, or tutor-dialogue explanation for the composition task.
ambiguities.current_json_handling: Set answer and explanation to null and marked the part as requiresManualReview.
